text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST — SURVEILLANCE POST-WHIPPLE
Also reviewed: Comparison with prior CT abdomen/pelvis (end of neoadjuvant chemoradiation, pre-operative)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old female with pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma, status post neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles) followed by consolidative chemoradiation (IMRT 50.4 Gy with concurrent cape), then pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) approximately 3 months prior. Now on adjuvant Gemzar/cape (cycle 3). Surveillance imaging for assessment of post-operative status, anastomotic integrity, and evaluation for disease recurrence. Known post-operative course complicated by grade B pancreatic fistula, now reportedly resolved clinically. Tumor markers pending.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed with intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350 mg I/mL, 120 mL administered via right antecubital peripheral IV at 3 mL/sec). Acquisitions obtained in the late arterial (pancreatic) phase, portal venous phase, and delayed venous phase following automated bolus triggering. No oral contrast administered. Axial images reconstructed at 2.5 mm thickness with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats generated. Radiation dose modulated. Images reviewed in conjunction with available prior CT examinations and operative report.

COMPARISON:
— CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (pre-surgical restaging, approximately 8 months prior to current study; roughly 2 months following completion of chemoradiation)
— CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (mid-treatment restaging after 4 cycles mFOLFIRINOX)
— Pre-treatment pancreatic protocol CT

FINDINGS:

SURGICAL BED AND PANCREAS:
The patient is status post pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy. Expected post-operative anatomy is noted with absence of the pancreatic head, uncinate process, duodenum, and gallbladder. The remnant pancreatic body and tail appear maintained in their native position without mass effect or morphologic change. The pancreatic neck anastomosis (pancreaticojejunostomy) is not discretely visualized as a distinct structure but the anastomotic region demonstrates expected soft tissue thickening and fibrous remodeling consistent with post-surgical change. No discrete mass lesion, irregular nodular enhancement, or soft tissue density suspicious for recurrent tumor is identified at the surgical bed or in the region of the prior primary mass. There is no abnormal enhancement in the peripancreatic fat that would suggest recurrent disease along the SMA margin or the retroperitoneal soft tissue plane.

A small, well-circumscribed fluid collection is present in the right upper quadrant adjacent to the pancreaticojejunostomy anastomotic site. This collection measures approximately 2.8 x 1.9 cm in its greatest axial dimensions (previously measured approximately 3.2 cm on the most recent prior scan available for comparison). The collection remains simple in appearance — it demonstrates homogeneous near-water-density contents (Hounsfield units approximately 8-14), with no internal gas locules, no internal septations or debris, and no peripheral rim enhancement to suggest an evolving abscess or infected collection. The surrounding soft tissue does not demonstrate inflammatory stranding beyond what is expected given prior fistula history and operative manipulation. Overall, this collection has decreased in size compared with the prior examination and its imaging characteristics are most consistent with a resolving post-surgical seroma or organizing fluid related to the prior grade B pancreatic fistula, now undergoing gradual resorption.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous parenchymal attenuation on all phases without focal lesions. No hypervascular or hypoattenuating hepatic nodules identified in arterial, portal venous, or delayed phases. No biloma or intrahepatic biliary dilatation. The intrahepatic bile ducts are decompressed, in expected fashion following biliary reconstruction. The hepaticojejunostomy is not directly visualized but no upstream biliary dilation is noted. No perihepatic fluid.

BILIARY TRACT:
The hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis is in keeping with post-operative configuration. The common hepatic duct and right and left hepatic ducts are not dilated. No periductal soft tissue thickening or enhancement raising concern for anastomotic stricture or recurrent disease at the biliary margin. No filling defects or biliary calculi.

STOMACH AND UPPER GASTROINTESTINAL:
The gastric remnant is decompressed and appears unremarkable. The gastrojejunostomy (duodenojejunostomy reconstruction) is not seen as a discrete structure on cross-sectional imaging. No gastric wall thickening, mucosal abnormality, or luminal obstruction is identified. Adjacent loops of jejunum appear patent.

SMALL AND LARGE BOWEL:
The visualized loops of small bowel and large bowel are not dilated and demonstrate normal wall thickness without evidence of obstruction, pneumatosis, or wall thickening. No free air is identified. Anastomotic loops adjacent to the hepaticojejunostomy and pancreaticojejunostomy demonstrate no mural thickening. No new mesenteric or omental nodularity suggesting peritoneal seeding.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
The superior mesenteric artery demonstrates a normal caliber and patent lumen without evidence of tumor encasement or periarterial soft tissue thickening at the site of prior tumor contact. No significant soft tissue along the SMA that would suggest recurrent disease. The superior mesenteric vein and portal vein confluence appear patent and of normal caliber without filling defects or wall thickening to suggest venous thrombosis or tumor recurrence. The celiac axis and its major branches (common hepatic artery, splenic artery) are patent. The aorta and inferior vena cava appear normal.

LYMPH NODES:
No enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the portocaval, celiac, periportal, peripancreatic, retroperitoneal, or mesenteric distributions. The largest visualized lymph node measures 0.5 cm in short axis in the portocaval region, below the threshold for pathologic enlargement and unchanged from prior. No new or suspicious adenopathy.

ADRENA L GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and morphology without nodularity or suspicious enhancement.

KIDNEYS AND URINARY TRACT:
Bilateral kidneys are of normal size and demonstrate symmetric nephrogram without hydronephrosis, calyceal dilatation, or renal masses. No urolithiasis. No perinephric collection or stranding. The urinary bladder appears normal.

SPLEEN:
The spleen is normal in size and attenuation without focal lesions.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity, omental caking, or serosal implants to suggest peritoneal metastatic disease. Mesenteric fat is unremarkable.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
The visualized osseous structures of the lower thoracic spine, lumbar spine, pelvis, and proximal femora demonstrate no lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for osseous metastasis. No pathologic fracture or cortical destruction. Abdominal wall and pelvic soft tissues are unremarkable. A small amount of subcutaneous fat is preserved. No hernia identified at the prior laparotomy incision site; the anterior abdominal wall fascial closure appears intact.

IMPRESSION:

1. STATUS POST PYLORUS-PRESERVING PANCREATICODUODENECTOMY — expected post-operative anatomical configuration with appropriate fibrous remodeling at the surgical bed. No evidence of locally recurrent pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma at the primary tumor site or along the prior margins of vascular contact (SMA, SMV territory).

2. ANASTOMOTIC FLUID COLLECTION — the small peri-anastomotic fluid collection adjacent to the pancreaticojejunostomy measures approximately 2.8 cm, representing a modest decrease in size compared with the prior available study (previously approximately 3.2 cm). The collection maintains simple, homogeneous fluid characteristics with no internal gas, debris, or peripheral enhancement. Imaging appearance is consistent with an organizing post-operative seroma or residual fluid from the prior grade B pancreatic fistula, continuing to undergo slow resolution. No features of superinfection or ongoing fistula at this time.

3. NO HEPATIC, PERITONEAL, NODAL, OR DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE — the liver, peritoneum, retroperitoneum, and mesentery are clear of any lesions suspicious for metastasis. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. No ascites.

4. PATENT VASCULAR RECONSTRUCTION — the SMA and SMV/portal confluence demonstrate no recurrent soft tissue encasement or thrombosis.

5. NO PULMONARY NODULES OR CHEST PATHOLOGY — note: CT chest was not included in this examination; dedicated chest imaging to be performed per surveillance protocol at discretion of treating oncologist.

Overall, this examination is reassuring for the absence of imaging evidence of pancreatic cancer recurrence at approximately 3 months following Whipple resection, in the setting of ongoing adjuvant chemotherapy. The anastomotic fluid collection continues to diminish in a manner consistent with expected post-surgical healing.

Reporting Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging Division]
Report status: Final
